_meta.title: Final Playbook content library - Q&A, action items, scenario-based education
_meta.purpose: Grounding data for the low-token AI agent: deterministic routing/rules only, no engineering/DDB/worksheet-validation content included by design (scoped to the clinical/educational routing logic per 2026-07-01 direction).
_meta.source: Extracted from niki_routing_rules_v1.html (6 Otter session transcripts: Peter Chmiel Sprint 1+2, Jayne Lammers, Ellen Feinberg, Sue, Billie), cross-checked against Elisa Seeherman meet-and-greet transcript for tone/glossary.
_meta.status: DRAFT — content and priority ordering pending expert sign-off. Use as working content-library seed; do not present as clinically final. Domain tags (added 2026-07-02) on actionItems and the definitionsGlossary array (added 2026-07-02) are engineering-authored classifications/definitions derived from this content for UI organization — not independently reviewed. Treat as a working scaffold, not clinically final; each glossary entry carries its own 'validated': false flag for tracking. Digital assessment questions D1-D3 (added 2026-07-02) are drafted from client-provided source questions, converted to fit this app's format, captured as MemberContext flags (noLegacyContact, noSocialMediaPlan, noPasswordManager) but NOT YET wired into lead-profile routing or action items, since no digital-gap action items have been authored yet. Unvalidated. profile_digital_gap (added 2026-07-02) is drafted, unvalidated content, practical digital-access how-tos matching the established voice/format, written to answer the digital-assessment gap (D1-D3). Always parallel/additive like profile_business_owner, never a lead profile. Each actionItem carries a `flag` field, rules_engine.build_plan() includes an item only if that specific MemberContext flag is true, not the whole profile at once.

coreFraming.philosophy: Break through the taboo of talking about death — but the empowerment is 'live fully, die ready.' Death is not a topic, it's an administrative and emotional reality that needs a plan.
keyLines: The worst time to plan a funeral is when someone's dead.
Debt is not dead anymore — there's a digital piece too.
It's not the sandwich generation anymore, it's the Panini generation — so squished in.
Acknowledge it: death is taboo, people avoid it, but not planning will cost time, money, and emotional stress when an event occurs.
How do we make it a roadmap rather than a treasure hunt?

threeScenarios.name: You die tomorrow
threeScenarios.focus: Your people taking action — they need documents, access, and a roadmap.
coreDocuments: Power of attorney — healthcare / medical advance directive
threeScenarios.quote: If you die tomorrow, there are three documents you absolutely need: a will, power of attorney, medical advance directive.

threeScenarios.name: You get a diagnosis
threeScenarios.focus: You're still alive but not able to make your own decisions — someone empowered to act on your known wishes.
elements: Healthcare proxy knows your wishes in detail
Trusted person empowered while you're incapacitated
threeScenarios.quote: Durable power of attorney for financial, durable power of attorney for healthcare — you're still alive, but you're not of sound mind, not making good decisions.

threeScenarios.name: Long-term care
threeScenarios.focus: Sustained care needs, usually last unless there's an active health urgency.
elements: Medicaid planning if assets are limited
threeScenarios.quote: If she needs to go into a facility, she doesn't have enough assets for that, so I'm going to work the Medicaid angle as she starts spinning down.

contextQuestions.question: Tell me about your family situation. Do you have a partner? Children — how old? Are your parents living? Do you have siblings?
contextQuestions.purpose: This single question determines the entire routing priority — which situation profile(s) apply.

contextQuestions.question: Is your parent living independently? Do they have the three core documents — will, power of attorney, and medical directive?
contextQuestions.purpose: If parent has no documents, parent planning gets prioritized above the member's own.

contextQuestions.question: What do you currently have in place? Will? Power of attorney? Medical directive? Have those been updated recently?
contextQuestions.purpose: Determines whether to start from scratch (clean slate) or pressure-test an existing plan.

contextQuestions.question: Have you had any major life changes in the last 5 years — divorce, marriage, new child, death of a named person, major asset change?
contextQuestions.purpose: If a post-divorce/major-change flag is set, beneficiary audit becomes first priority regardless of everything else.

contextQuestions.question: Are you currently the primary caregiver for someone else? Do you own or co-own a business?
contextQuestions.purpose: Each adds a separate parallel checklist to the member's plan.

contextQuestions.question: What's the best way for you to track this? Notebook, spreadsheet, Google Doc, app? How do we keep you accountable?
contextQuestions.purpose: Determines how action items should be delivered/tracked back to the member.

contextQuestions.question: If you died tomorrow and your phone went with you, is there a Legacy Contact set up on your account so someone could actually get in?
contextQuestions.purpose: Flags whether the member has any digital-access plan at all for their phone/accounts.

options.label: Yes, it's set up
Maybe — not sure
No

contextQuestions.question: Have you named someone to handle your social media if you couldn't do it yourself — and do they actually know what to do?
contextQuestions.purpose: Flags whether social media has a named person AND real instructions, not just a name.

options.label: Yes — named, and they have instructions
Named, but no instructions yet
No

contextQuestions.question: Are your passwords and logins somewhere a trusted person could actually find and use — a password manager, anything real?
contextQuestions.purpose: Flags whether login/credential access exists anywhere outside the member's own head.

options.label: Yes
No

peopleBeforeDocuments.principle: The method never starts with 'you need a will.' It starts with 'who are the people in your life?' — abstract legal concepts become concrete by attaching real names first, then mapping people to document roles.
peopleBeforeDocuments.quote: Those people you identify as emergency contacts — the question is, are they going to be in your will? Are they going to be the executor, power of attorney, medical advance directive?

mapping.question: Who would you call if something happened to you tonight? Does that person know they're your first call?
If you died, who takes the first action? That person is probably your executor.
Who do you trust most with your financial decisions if you can't make them?
Who knows your healthcare wishes? Who would you trust to make those decisions?
If something happened to both you and your spouse, who takes the kids? Does that person know?
If you were gone tomorrow, who runs the business? Do they have what they need?
Does the neighbor/caregiver have your phone number? Could they reach you if something happened?

situationProfiles.name: Aging parent with limited documentation
questions: Does your parent have a will, power of attorney (financial + healthcare), and medical advance directive?
Do YOU have copies of those documents?
Do you know if they have a DNR? Where is it physically kept?
Does whoever would respond in an emergency (neighbor, building super, aide) have YOUR contact information?
Do you have a key to their home? Do you know the security code?
Is there a safe deposit box? Are you authorized on it?
Are you and your sibling(s) both named as co-executors or co-POA holders?
When was the will last updated? Does it reflect current wishes?
priorityOrder: Parent documents BEFORE member's own — get will, POA financial, POA healthcare, medical directive from parent's lawyer
Confirm DNR status and physical location
Emergency access — neighbor's number, house key, security code
Safe deposit box — are you authorized?
Member's own three documents (deferred until parent situation is addressed)

actionItems.text: Ask your parent for copies of their will, power of attorney, and medical advance directive.
actionItems.script: Mom, I'm doing my estate planning. I realized I don't have a copy of yours. If something happened to you tomorrow, I would be responsible. Can you give me a copy of your will, power of attorney, and medical advance directive?

actionItems.text: Ask: Do you have a DNR? Where is it kept? Check likely spots — near front door, refrigerator, hospital copy.
Get the neighbor's / building manager's contact info. Give them YOUR number.
Find the house key. Know the security code. Make sure a sibling also has access.
Call the bank: is my name on her safe deposit box? If not, how do we add it?
Ask your parent when their will was last updated and whether it still reflects who they want.

situationProfiles.quote: I just don't want you to get the call and not be able to act.

situationProfiles.name: Young children, no guardian named
questions: If something happened to both you and your spouse/partner tonight, who takes the kids?
Does that person know they would be named? Have they agreed?
Have you and your spouse agreed on the same person?
Does that person have the capacity — financially, physically, emotionally?
Is there life insurance adequate to fund raising your children to adulthood?
Does your spouse's family know what your wishes are for the children?
situationProfiles.specialNote: Do NOT name minor children directly as beneficiaries on accounts — assets need a trust or UTMA custodial account to avoid court control until the child reaches adulthood.
priorityOrder: Guardian conversation between spouses — agree on one person (and a backup)
Confirm with the named guardian that they know and agree
Will with guardian named + executor named
Life insurance review — is coverage enough to raise them?
Trust or custodial account consideration (assets shouldn't pass directly to minors)
Emergency contact card for schools, activities, pediatrician

actionItems.text: Have the conversation with your partner: who is the guardian? Agree before next session.
Contact that person and ask directly.
actionItems.script: We'd like to name you as guardian for our children. Are you willing and prepared?

actionItems.text: See an estate attorney: will + guardian named + executor named (same or different person).
Review life insurance: is the amount adequate to cover child-rearing through age 22?
Confirm extended family knows the plan.
actionItems.script: If something happened to us both, who takes the kids? Does your wife's sister know? Have you had that conversation?

situationProfiles.quote: If something happened to us both, who takes the kids? Does your wife's sister know? Have you had that conversation?

situationProfiles.name: Solo ager (no partner, no children)
questions: Who would you call if something happened to you tonight?
Does that person know they're your first call?
Do you have a healthcare proxy who knows your wishes in detail?
Are all your accounts set up with transfer on death (TOD) or payable on death (POD)?
Have you thought about who gets your assets — people or organizations?
Do you have IP, a business, or a website that needs a shutdown plan?
Are you the informal caregiver for a parent with limited assets? Have you thought about Medicaid planning?
priorityOrder: Transfer on death (TOD) on all brokerage accounts + payable on death (POD) on all bank accounts — prevents probate without a natural heir
Healthcare proxy who truly knows your wishes (not just named on paper)
Professional fiduciary consideration for financial decisions if no trusted person
Digital legacy — business shutdown plan, IP documentation, account access
Charitable/organizational beneficiary conversations
Childless trust consideration if assets are significant

actionItems.text: Call each financial institution.
actionItems.script: I'm calling to say I'm doing estate planning. I just want to make sure: do I have transfer on death set up on this account? If not, how do we set that up?

actionItems.text: Sit with your healthcare proxy and walk through your actual wishes, not just 'I trust you to decide.'
Document your digital legacy: the accounts, the business, the clients. Who shuts it down? What are the instructions?
Use an online notary for remaining documents.
Freeze your credit at all three bureaus.
actionItems.quote: If you don't need your credit available right now — not buying a car, not buying a house — your credit should be frozen. It's the best protection against the data breaches happening right now.

situationProfiles.quote: There's actually a niche market — childless trusts. They only deal with people who have no children, but they have assets. They assign fiduciaries, designate organizations, decide on distribution terms.

situationProfiles.name: Business owner / entrepreneur
questions: If something happened to you tomorrow, who runs the business?
Does that person have the credentials, logins, and access they need?
Do you have key person insurance?
Do you have old 401k accounts from previous employers that haven't been rolled over?
Are your IP, patents, or content assets documented and owned clearly?
Are your corporate/founding documents (incorporation, operating agreement) accessible?
situationProfiles.criticalRule: Business plan and personal plan are two separate checklists. Your personal life would be fine. Your business life — that's a different question.
priorityOrder: Name the business successor / 'business executor' — who takes over
Key person insurance review
401k rollover from any previous employers (extremely common gap among founders who left corporate jobs)
Business contact document: vendors, clients, accounts — separate from personal
IP ownership documentation
Corporate documents in a crucial doc box (separate section from personal documents)

actionItems.text: Identify the business successor and brief them.
Research or review key person insurance.
Locate all old 401k accounts from previous employers and initiate rollover.
Create a business contacts document (clients, key vendors, accounts, login instructions) — separate from personal documents.
Add corporate documents to your crucial doc box as a separate section.

situationProfiles.quote: A lot of founders left their corporate jobs and have 401ks that need to be rolled over. They left their ex-spouse on as a beneficiary — oh yeah.

situationProfiles.name: Post-divorce or major life change in last 5 years
situationProfiles.urgency: beneficiary audit is first action before all else
questions: Have you checked ALL beneficiary designations since that change? (Each account separately — they do not update automatically)
401k, IRA, life insurance, brokerage — on each one: who is listed?
Is an ex-spouse still listed anywhere? (More common than people expect)
Has your will been updated since the life change?
Have the people named in your POA and healthcare proxy changed?
priorityOrder: Audit ALL beneficiary designations: 401k, IRA, brokerage (TOD), life insurance, pension
Update will to reflect current wishes and current named persons
Update POA and healthcare proxy if named persons have changed
Review assets acquired since the change: do they have the right designations?

actionItems.text: List every account with a beneficiary designation. Pull the form or call the institution. Verify who is listed. Update immediately if wrong.
Check specifically whether an ex-spouse is still listed anywhere — retirement account beneficiary designations override what the will says.
Schedule time with an estate attorney to update the will.
Check whether the people named in your POA are still the right people.

quotes: They left their ex-spouse on as a beneficiary of their 401k after 20 years. Oh yeah. I didn't even think about that.
She had a wealth manager but she had no will. She died intestate. The adult children are scrambling — they can't access her accounts. It has to go through probate.

situationProfiles.name: Nothing done, clean slate
situationProfiles.approach: No judgment, no urgency panic. You don't have a plan because life gets busy — it's not prioritized, that's why it takes time. Start with ONE concrete physical action. Give the full scope so they can see the project, then sequence it themselves.
questions: Who would be the first person called if something happened to you tonight?
Do you know where your birth certificate is?
If your partner died tomorrow, could you find all the financial accounts?
Is there a fireproof box somewhere with your important documents?
priorityOrder: Emergency contact card — who are the people? (seeds all document decisions)
Asset inventory — what do you have? Update beneficiary column on each account.
Crucial doc box — find a fireproof box, identify what goes in it
Three core documents — will, POA financial, POA healthcare / medical directive
Digital vault setup (deferred until physical is established)

actionItems.text: Print and fill out emergency contact cards.
Find and update your asset inventory spreadsheet. Add a beneficiary column, TOD/POD status, and a point of contact.
Make ONE call to ONE financial institution this week.
actionItems.script: Am I set up with TOD/POD on this account?

actionItems.text: Find your birth certificate (the gateway physical-document exercise).
Have one conversation with your spouse about who would be the guardian for the kids.

situationProfiles.quote: How do we make it a roadmap rather than a treasure hunt?

situationProfiles.name: Has some documents, needs pressure testing
situationProfiles.approach: You've done work, but have you pressure-tested it? Review what exists, identify what's outdated, find what's incomplete.
questions: When was this document last updated?
Do you have a digital copy? Can you get one from your lawyer?
Does your executor / POA person know they're named? Do they understand what that means?
Are the named people still the right people? Has anything changed?
Is the HIPAA release embedded in your healthcare proxy, or separate?
Do you have a DNR as a standalone document (not just referenced in the living will)?
Have you reviewed beneficiary designations recently?
priorityOrder: Get digital copies of all documents from the lawyer
Re-read the documents — don't assume you know what they say
Call executor / POA to confirm they know they're named and what to do
Identify the specific gap (standalone DNR, trust, updated beneficiaries, HIPAA)
Address the gap with the same lawyer or a new one

actionItems.text: Call your lawyer.
actionItems.script: Can you email me the digital versions of my will, POA, and medical directive?

actionItems.text: Read each document. Note anything that surprises you or that you want to change.
Call your executor / POA person.
actionItems.script: I want to make sure you know you're named and understand the responsibilities.

actionItems.text: Check the beneficiary on each account — especially after any life change.
Identify the gap document and schedule time to address it.

situationProfiles.quote: Matthew Perry had a trust, but he left one bank account out of the trust — it had $1.1M. Because he left it out, the trust had to go through probate. The old girlfriend was the beneficiary, not the new one. He never updated.

situationProfiles.name: Digital access gaps
questions: If you died tomorrow and your phone went with you, is there a Legacy Contact set up on your account so someone could actually get in?
Have you named someone to handle your social media if you couldn't do it yourself, and do they actually know what to do?
Are your passwords and logins somewhere a trusted person could actually find and use?
priorityOrder: Legacy Contact on phone/device accounts, the gateway to everything else digital
Named social media executor with real instructions, not just a name
Password manager or written access list a trusted person can actually use

actionItems.text: Set up a Legacy Contact on your phone account, so someone you trust could actually get in if something happened to you.
Name someone to handle your social media if you couldn't, and write down exactly what you want done (memorialize, delete, or keep active).
actionItems.script: If something happened to me, here's what I'd want you to do with my accounts, can I walk you through it?

actionItems.text: Put your passwords in a password manager, and make sure the person who'd need access knows how to get in.

quantityCap.rule: Maximum 5 action items per sprint/session. More kills momentum.
quantityCap.quote: I can give you a checklist and you'd be like, but we don't get it done for multiple reasons.
quantityCap.checkPrevious: Always ask first: 'What did you complete from last time?' before adding new items.

calendarAnchoring.rule: Every action item is tied to the member's real calendar, not an arbitrary deadline.
examples: Before you go to Florida (4-day urgency window)
This month — no rock climbing, no founder institute (leverage an open calendar)

noJudgmentFraming.rule: When items aren't completed, don't shame — normalize.
noJudgmentFraming.quote: Life gets busy. It's not prioritized. That's why it takes time. There's no judgment.

sequencingRule.rule: People → Documents → Access, in that order, every time.
steps: Identify the PEOPLE (who is the executor, POA, guardian, healthcare proxy)
Create the DOCUMENTS that formalize those roles
Ensure ACCESS (crucial doc box, digital vault, account access instructions)

conversationScripts.note: These give exact language for difficult conversations. Reuse these verbatim where the profile matches — don't paraphrase, the phrasing is deliberately low-friction/non-confrontational.

scripts.context: Approaching an aging parent for documents
scripts.script: Mom, I'm doing my estate planning. I realized I don't have a copy of yours. If something happened to you tomorrow, I would be responsible. Can you give me a copy of your will, power of attorney, and medical advance directive?

scripts.context: Calling a financial institution about TOD/POD
scripts.script: I'm calling to say I'm doing estate planning. I just want to make sure: do I have transfer on death set up on this account? If not, how do we set that up?
If you don't need your credit available right now — not buying a car, not buying a house — your credit should be frozen. It's the best protection against the data breaches happening right now.

voiceAndGlossary.source: Elisa Seeherman meet-and-greet transcript — tone/vocabulary reference only, not an action-item source (this was a business-development conversation, not a coaching session).

terms.note: The deliverable/gift a member builds — something to hand to loved ones.
Replaces 'sandwich generation' — squished between caring for kids and aging parents.
Die tomorrow unexpectedly / terminal diagnosis / long-term care plan.

voiceAndGlossary.illustrativeStory: Elisa's father recorded a video interview with his mother-in-law and posted it to YouTube; YouTube's settings don't allow official downloads, and the original digital file's location is unknown. If the YouTube account or platform goes away, the family loses the video permanently — a concrete example of why digital-asset planning (not just legal/financial) matters.

definitionsGlossary.shortDefinition: A legal document that names your executor and states who receives your assets after you die.
A legal document that lets a trusted person make financial decisions on your behalf while you're alive but unable to make them yourself.

definitionsGlossary.term: Power of attorney (healthcare) / medical advance directive
definitionsGlossary.shortDefinition: A legal document naming who can make medical decisions for you if you can't, and stating your care wishes in detail.
A medical order stating you do not want CPR performed if your heart stops or you stop breathing.
The person legally responsible for carrying out your will after you die — settling accounts, distributing assets, closing the estate.
A designation on a brokerage or investment account that names who receives it directly when you die, skipping probate.
Like transfer-on-death, but for bank accounts — names who receives the account balance directly when you die.
The person or people named on an account (401k, life insurance, IRA) to receive it after you die. These override what your will says, and don't update automatically.
The court process of settling an estate when accounts or property don't have a named beneficiary or designation — slower and more public than a direct transfer.
Your online accounts, files, and business logins — who has access, and what happens to them, if you're gone.
The person legally named to raise your minor children if you (and your partner) are no longer able to.

A legal arrangement where a trustee holds and manages assets for your beneficiaries. Unlike a will, a living trust can let assets pass to your people WITHOUT going through probate court, and can take effect while you're alive if you become unable to manage things yourself.
definitionsGlossary._note: DRAFT 2026-07-12: plain-language term for Jesse, educational only, not advice. Pending Niki review.

definitionsGlossary.shortDefinition: A will takes effect only after you die and usually goes through probate (a public court process) before assets are distributed. A living trust can avoid probate, works if you're incapacitated (not just at death), and stays private. Many people have BOTH: a will as a backstop, and a trust for the main assets. Which fits you depends on your situation. An estate attorney can advise.
definitionsGlossary._note: DRAFT 2026-07-12: plain-language term for Jesse, educational only, not advice. Pending Niki review.

definitionsGlossary.shortDefinition: A document that states the medical care you do or don't want if you can't speak for yourself (for example, life support). Different from a regular will, which is about your assets after death.
definitionsGlossary._note: DRAFT 2026-07-12: plain-language term for Jesse, educational only, not advice. Pending Niki review.

definitionsGlossary.shortDefinition: Everything you own at death: property, accounts, belongings, and digital assets. Your estate is what gets distributed by your will, trust, or, if you have neither, by state law.
definitionsGlossary._note: DRAFT 2026-07-12: plain-language term for Jesse, educational only, not advice. Pending Niki review.

definitionsGlossary.shortDefinition: Dying without a valid will. When that happens, your state's laws decide who inherits your assets, which may not match what you would have wanted.
definitionsGlossary._note: DRAFT 2026-07-12: plain-language term for Jesse, educational only, not advice. Pending Niki review.

definitionsGlossary.shortDefinition: The person or organization you name to receive an asset (like a retirement account or life insurance) when you die. Beneficiary designations usually override what your will says, so keeping them current matters.
definitionsGlossary._note: DRAFT 2026-07-12: plain-language term for Jesse, educational only, not advice. Pending Niki review.

definitionsGlossary.shortDefinition: A policy that pays a chosen beneficiary a sum of money when you die. It can help your family cover costs and replace lost income. Confirm the named beneficiary is still who you intend.
definitionsGlossary._note: DRAFT 2026-07-12: plain-language term for Jesse, educational only, not advice. Pending Niki review.

definitionsGlossary.shortDefinition: A single organized list of your accounts, policies, debts, and where to find them, so the person handling your affairs isn't searching blind.
definitionsGlossary._note: DRAFT 2026-07-12: plain-language term for Jesse, educational only, not advice. Pending Niki review.

definitionsGlossary.shortDefinition: The person you legally name to make medical decisions for you if you can't make them yourself. Also called a healthcare power of attorney.
definitionsGlossary._note: DRAFT 2026-07-12: plain-language term for Jesse, educational only, not advice. Pending Niki review.

definitionsGlossary.shortDefinition: A general term for the documents that spell out your medical wishes and who decides for you: typically a living will plus a healthcare proxy.
definitionsGlossary._note: DRAFT 2026-07-12: plain-language term for Jesse, educational only, not advice. Pending Niki review.

definitionsGlossary.shortDefinition: A widely used, plain-language advance directive that covers medical, personal, emotional, and spiritual wishes, not just legal ones.
definitionsGlossary._note: DRAFT 2026-07-12: plain-language term for Jesse, educational only, not advice. Pending Niki review.

definitionsGlossary.shortDefinition: A person you designate with Apple, Google, or Facebook who can access or manage your account after you die. Setting one up is one of the simplest, highest-impact digital steps.
definitionsGlossary._note: DRAFT 2026-07-12: plain-language term for Jesse, educational only, not advice. Pending Niki review.

definitionsGlossary.shortDefinition: A secure app that stores your logins in one place. It lets a trusted person reach your accounts (with the right access) instead of being locked out.
definitionsGlossary._note: DRAFT 2026-07-12: plain-language term for Jesse, educational only, not advice. Pending Niki review.

definitionsGlossary.shortDefinition: Anything you own or control online: accounts, photos, email, social media, crypto, domains, and files. Without a plan, much of it can be lost or inaccessible to your family.
definitionsGlossary._note: DRAFT 2026-07-12: plain-language term for Jesse, educational only, not advice. Pending Niki review.

definitionsGlossary.shortDefinition: Google's tool to decide what happens to your account and data if you stop using it, including who can be notified or given access.
definitionsGlossary._note: DRAFT 2026-07-12: plain-language term for Jesse, educational only, not advice. Pending Niki review.

definitionsGlossary.shortDefinition: A personal (non-legal) letter sharing your values, stories, and wishes for your loved ones. Not legally binding, but often the most treasured thing you leave behind.
definitionsGlossary._note: DRAFT 2026-07-12: plain-language term for Jesse, educational only, not advice. Pending Niki review.

domainAssessment._note: Condensed consumer model derived from the full domain source in content-sources/04-domain-notes/. One action per answer; steps are shown behind a 'show me how' expander. DRAFT, pending expert sign-off.

basicsFirst.title: Set up your doc box
basicsFirst.action: Create one central, secure place ('doc box') where all your important documents live, so everything else can actually be found.
steps: Pick one secure location (a fireproof/waterproof box, or a single encrypted cloud folder). One place, not five.
Put your key documents there as you complete each step below.
Tell your trusted person it exists and how to reach it.

basicsFirst.title: Set up a Legacy Contact on your phone (iPhone & Android)
basicsFirst.action: Add a Legacy Contact so your trusted person can reach your device and accounts. It's free and takes 2 minutes, on whichever phone you use.
steps: iPhone: Settings > tap your name > Sign-In & Security > Legacy Contact > Add Legacy Contact.
Android / Google: myaccount.google.com > Data & privacy > 'Make a plan for your account' (Inactive Account Manager).
Choose the person you trust to handle your device and accounts.
Tell them they've been named and what it's for.

questions.question: If something happened tomorrow, would the person managing your money know exactly which financial institutions to call?

options.label: Yes, they have the names and contacts in writing
options.action: Review your financial contact list so it still reflects your current accounts and institutions.
checklist: Are all institutions still correct and active?
Have you opened or closed any accounts recently?
Are the contact names and phone numbers still accurate?
Does your agent know where this list is stored?

options.label: They know some institutions but not all the contacts
options.action: Complete your financial contact list so your agent has everything they need in an emergency.
steps: Gather all your financial institutions (banks, credit unions, investment and retirement accounts, insurance).
Add contact names, phone numbers, and departments for each.
Update the list with any new accounts or changes.
Give your agent a copy and tell them where the original is stored.

options.label: No
options.action: Create a complete list of your financial institutions and contacts so your agent can act without delays.
steps: Write down every financial institution you use.
Add contact names, phone numbers, and departments.
Store the list somewhere secure and accessible.
Give your agent a copy so they can act immediately if needed.

questions.question: Have you named, and recently checked, the beneficiaries on your 401(k), IRA, and bank/investment accounts?

options.label: Yes, all named and reviewed in the last year or two
options.action: Review your beneficiary designations. These override your will, so a stale one sends money to the wrong person.
checklist: Is every retirement account (401k, IRA) beneficiary current?
Are bank and investment account beneficiaries (TOD/POD) set?
Did a marriage, divorce, birth, or death change who you'd name?
Do you have both a primary AND a contingent beneficiary on each?

options.label: Some are named, but I haven't checked them in years
options.action: Review and update your beneficiary designations, they override your will, so out-of-date ones cause the biggest, most avoidable mistakes.
steps: List every account that names a beneficiary (401k, IRA, pension, bank, brokerage, life insurance).
Log in or call each institution and confirm who's named.
Add or fix a primary and a contingent beneficiary on each.
Re-check after any big life change (marriage, divorce, birth, death).

options.label: No, or I'm not sure
options.action: Name beneficiaries on your accounts. This is one of the highest-impact steps, beneficiary designations pass money directly, skipping probate.
steps: List your 401k, IRA, bank, and investment accounts.
For each, ask the institution how to add a beneficiary (often called TOD/POD on bank/brokerage accounts).
Name a primary and a contingent (backup) beneficiary.
Keep a note of who you named with your other documents.

questions.question: If you couldn't speak for yourself in a medical emergency, have you written down the care you would and wouldn't want, so doctors and your family aren't left guessing?

options.label: Yes, signed and on file with my doctor and Healthcare POA
options.action: Review your Advance Directive so it still reflects your current wishes.
checklist: Is your directive still exactly what you want?
Does your doctor have the most recent version?
Does your Healthcare POA know where the original is stored?
Have related documents (HIPAA release, POLST, DNR) been updated?

options.label: I've started one but it isn't signed or distributed
options.action: Finish and distribute your Advance Directive so it can actually guide your care.
steps: Finish writing it, what treatments you do and don't want (ventilation, resuscitation, feeding tubes, comfort-only care).
Sign it per your state's requirements (witnesses or notary).
Give copies to your doctor, hospital system, and Healthcare POA.
Store the original securely and tell your POA where it is.

options.label: No
options.action: Create a Medical Advance Directive so doctors know your wishes if you can't speak for yourself.
steps: Decide what interventions you'd want or decline (CPR, ventilation, feeding tubes, comfort-only care).
Write your choices in a directive form for your state.
Sign with required witnesses or a notary.
Give copies to your doctor, hospital, and Healthcare POA; store the original securely.

questions.question: If something happened tomorrow, could your trusted person get into your accounts, do you use a password manager they can reach?

options.label: Yes, I use one and emergency access is set up
options.action: Review your password manager so your critical accounts and emergency access are current.
checklist: Are your major accounts (email, banking, cloud) all in it?
Are recovery codes and 2FA backups stored?
Is emergency access set up and assigned to the right person?
Is your master password written down somewhere secure?

options.label: I use one, but no one else has a way in
options.action: Give your trusted person a secure way into your password manager.
steps: Set up emergency access (1Password/Bitwarden/Dashlane) or a Legacy Contact if supported.
Write your master password down and store it securely (safe, sealed envelope, attorney's file).
Document one simple page of unlock instructions.
Tell your trusted person where that information is stored.

options.label: No, I don't use a password manager
options.action: Start using a password manager so your logins are secure and reachable if someone needs to step in.
steps: Choose a password manager (1Password, Bitwarden, Apple Keychain).
Add your most important accounts first (email, banking, cloud).
Turn on 2FA and store the recovery codes in the manager.
Set up emergency access or store your master password securely, and tell your trusted person how to reach it.

questions.question: Could your trusted person actually get into your online banking and bill-pay if they had to, not just know the bank's name, but log in?

options.label: Yes, logins and 2FA access are documented for them
options.action: Review your online-banking access so logins and two-factor methods are still current.
checklist: Are all online banking / bill-pay logins captured?
Are 2FA methods (texts, authenticator apps) accounted for?
Are autopay and scheduled bills documented so nothing lapses?
Does your trusted person know where these are stored?

options.label: They know the banks but couldn't actually log in
options.action: Document real login access to your online banking, including the two-factor step that usually blocks people.
steps: List each bank's online login (username + where the password lives).
Note the 2FA method for each, and how your person would receive the code (this is the part that traps families).
List autopay / scheduled payments so bills don't lapse.
Store this in your password manager and tell your person where it is.

options.label: No, they'd be locked out
options.action: Set up a way for your trusted person to reach your online banking so bills and accounts don't freeze up in an emergency.
steps: Put each bank login into your password manager.
Write down the 2FA method for each and how a trusted person could get the code.
List recurring/autopay bills so they can keep them current.
Give your trusted person access to the manager or the sealed instructions.

questions.question: Are your photos, videos, and personal files backed up, AND does your family know how to get to them?

options.label: Yes, backed up and access is documented
options.action: Review your backups so they're current and your family can reach them.
checklist: Are backups current (cloud and/or an external drive)?
Are the access details (logins, drive location) accurate?
Are those credentials in your password manager?
Does your trusted person know where the backup lives?

options.label: Backed up, but no one knows how to access them
options.action: Make sure someone can actually find and open your photos and files, a backup no one can reach is lost with you.
steps: Confirm photos/videos/files are backed up (cloud and/or external drive).
Write down how to access each backup (logins, physical drive location, any app needed).
Store those access details in your password manager.
Tell your trusted person where the instructions are.

options.label: No, not really backed up
options.action: Back up your photos and files and leave a way in, so a lifetime of memories isn't lost.
steps: Choose a cloud service (iCloud, Google Photos, OneDrive) and/or an external drive.
Back up your photos, videos, and important files.
Document how to access each backup and store logins in your manager.
Tell your trusted person where the backup and instructions are.

questions.question: Do you own valuable digital assets, like an online business, domain names, creative work, cryptocurrency, or online accounts worth money, and could the right person actually access and inherit them?

options.label: Yes, documented with a secure access plan
options.action: Review your digital-asset access so wallet keys and logins are current and reachable.
checklist: Are all assets still listed (wallets, NFTs, domains, points)?
Are wallet keys / recovery phrases / logins up to date?
Are they stored securely (manager or secure physical location)?
Does your trusted person know how to access them?

options.label: I own some, but no one could access them
options.action: Document access to your digital assets, crypto especially is gone forever without the keys, no bank can recover it.
steps: List every digital asset (crypto wallets, NFTs, domains, online business, loyalty points).
Document access per asset, wallet keys, recovery phrases, logins, transfer steps.
Store this in a password manager or a secure physical location.
Tell your trusted person where it is and how to use it.

options.label: I don't own any
options.action: No action needed now, but re-check yearly. Digital assets accumulate quietly (a wallet, domains, loyalty points).
checklist: Do a yearly check: any new wallet, domain, or online asset?
If yes, document access the same way as other accounts.

questions.question: Have you decided what happens to your social media accounts (Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, etc.), memorialize, delete, or transfer?

options.label: Yes, accounts listed and decisions documented
options.action: Review your social-media plan so accounts and decisions are still current.
checklist: Are all accounts listed and still active?
Do your memorialize/delete/transfer choices still hold?
Are logins in your password manager?
Does your trusted person know where the plan is kept?

options.label: Some listed, but no decisions made
options.action: Decide what happens to each social account so your online presence isn't left in limbo.
steps: List every social account, including older/rarely used ones.
Decide for each: memorialize, delete, transfer, or archive.
Note where logins are stored (password manager).
Store the list securely and tell your trusted person where it is.

options.label: No
options.action: Document your social accounts and what should happen to each.
steps: List every account across all platforms.
Decide memorialize / delete / transfer for each.
Put logins in your password manager.
Tell your trusted person where your instructions are.

questions.question: Have you named the specific person who's legally allowed to handle your digital accounts after death (a 'Digital Asset Manager')?

options.label: Yes, named in writing and authorized in my will
options.action: Review your Digital Asset Manager designation so it still matches your digital life and your wishes.
checklist: Is the named person still the right choice?
Is the authority stated in your will or estate documents?
Do they know the role and what it involves?
Can they find your access instructions?

options.label: I've picked someone but nothing's in writing
options.action: Give your chosen person written, legal authority, without it, platforms and courts can block them from your accounts.
steps: Add a Digital Asset Manager designation to your will or estate plan.
Write clear instructions for accessing your digital accounts.
Store them securely and make sure your person knows where they are.
Review your digital-asset list so it includes everything they'll need.

options.label: No
options.action: Name a Digital Asset Manager so your online accounts can be handled properly instead of frozen or lost.
steps: Choose a trusted person who's comfortable with technology.
Add their role to your will or estate documents for legal authority.
Document your digital accounts and access instructions.
Store it securely and tell them where to find it.

questions._note: DRAFT (2026-07-08): Claude-authored scenario question, matches existing voice/format. Educational situation-surfacing only, not advice. Pending Niki sign-off.
questions.question: If both parents were gone tomorrow, is there a legally named guardian who would raise your children?

options.label: Yes, a guardian is named in a signed document
options.action: Review your guardian choice so it still reflects who you'd want and their current willingness.
checklist: Is the named guardian still the right person and still willing?
Have you named a backup guardian if the first can't serve?
Does the guardian know they're named, and where the document is?
Has anything changed (a move, a falling-out, a new child) since you named them?

options.label: We've talked about it, but nothing is written down
options.action: Put your guardian choice in writing so a court would honor it, instead of deciding for your children.
steps: Decide on a first-choice guardian and a backup, and confirm both are willing.
Have the choice documented in your will or a standalone guardian nomination (an estate attorney can do this).
Tell the guardians where the document is kept.
Revisit the choice after any major family change.

options.label: No, no guardian has been named
options.action: Name a guardian for your children so the choice is yours, not a court's.
steps: Talk with your first-choice guardian (and a backup) to confirm they'd say yes.
Have the nomination documented in your will or a guardian designation.
Store it in your doc box and tell the guardian it exists.
Note any wishes about how you'd want the children raised.

questions._note: DRAFT (2026-07-08): Claude-authored scenario question, matches existing voice/format. Educational situation-surfacing only, not advice. Pending Niki sign-off.
questions.question: If you were gone, is there money set aside so whoever raises your children isn't doing it out of pocket?

options.label: Yes, life insurance and/or a trust is in place
options.action: Review that the provision for your children is current and points to the right people.
checklist: Is the coverage amount still enough for their care and education?
Are the beneficiaries (or a trust) named so a minor doesn't inherit directly?
Does the guardian know how the funds are accessed?
Have you updated it after any new child or major change?

options.label: No, there's no plan for their financial care
options.action: Set up a way for your children to be provided for if you're gone.
steps: Estimate what raising them to adulthood would take (care, housing, education).
Explore term life insurance to cover that gap.
Ask an estate attorney whether a trust should receive the funds (so a minor doesn't inherit directly).
Make sure the guardian knows the plan and how to reach the funds.

questions._note: DRAFT (2026-07-08): Claude-authored scenario question, matches existing voice/format. Educational situation-surfacing only, not advice. Pending Niki sign-off.
questions.question: If you couldn't run your business tomorrow, is there a written plan for who takes over and how?

options.label: Yes, there's a documented succession plan
options.action: Review your succession plan so it matches how the business runs today.
checklist: Is the named successor still willing and able?
Are ownership-transfer terms (buy-sell, valuation) still current?
Do key people know the plan exists and where to find it?
Has anything changed (partners, structure, value) since you wrote it?

options.label: There's an idea, but nothing formal
options.action: Turn your succession idea into a written plan so the business survives a sudden gap.
steps: Name who would take over day-to-day operations, and a backup.
Document how ownership transfers (a buy-sell agreement, if there are partners).
Write down the essentials only you know: key accounts, vendors, logins, passwords.
Tell your successor where the plan and the credentials live.

options.label: No, the business depends entirely on me
options.action: Create a basic continuity plan so your business isn't lost if you can't run it.
steps: Write down the handful of things that would stop if you disappeared (accounts, contracts, access).
Name one person who could step in, even temporarily.
Put critical logins and documents in your doc box, reachable by that person.
Consult an attorney about ownership transfer and a buy-sell if you have partners.

questions._note: DRAFT (2026-07-08): Claude-authored scenario question, matches existing voice/format. Educational situation-surfacing only, not advice. Pending Niki sign-off.
questions.question: If you couldn't speak for yourself in a medical crisis, does one specific person have the legal authority to decide for you?

options.label: Yes, a healthcare proxy/agent is named in writing
options.action: Review your healthcare proxy so the right person can act without friction.
checklist: Is the named agent still the right person and still willing?
Have you named a backup if they're unreachable?
Does your agent know your wishes about care, not just that they're named?
Do your doctor and hospital have a copy on file?

options.label: No, no one is formally authorized
options.action: Name a healthcare agent so someone you trust can decide for you in a crisis.
steps: Choose the person who'd make medical decisions the way you'd want, and a backup.
Complete a healthcare power of attorney / proxy form for your state.
Talk with your agent about what matters to you, so they're not guessing.
Give copies to your agent, your doctor, and your doc box.

questions._note: DRAFT (2026-07-09): Claude-authored scenario question, matches existing voice/format. Educational situation-surfacing only, not advice. Pending Niki sign-off.
questions.question: After losing someone close, was there something they didn't leave behind that made it harder — access, wishes, or documents?

options.label: Yes — and I don't want that for my people
options.action: Capture the specific gaps you saw, so your own plan closes them first.
steps: Write down what was missing when you settled or grieved (a password, a wish, a document).
Mark which of those you haven't handled for yourself yet.
Start with the one that caused the most friction.
Put it in your doc box so your people never hit that wall.

options.label: It was hard, but I'm not sure what was missing
options.action: Review the moments that were hardest, to surface what your own plan should include.
checklist: What information did you or the family scramble to find?
Were their wishes clear, or did people have to guess?
Could you access what you needed (accounts, documents, the home)?
Which of those are unresolved in your own life?

questions._note: DRAFT (2026-07-09): Claude-authored scenario question, matches existing voice/format. Educational situation-surfacing only, not advice. Pending Niki sign-off.
questions.question: Having settled an estate, do you have your own executor named — and have you spared them what you went through?

options.label: Yes — my executor is named and equipped
options.action: Review that your executor has what you wished you'd had.
checklist: Is your executor named in a current, signed document?
Do they know where every account and document lives?
Have you left the instructions you wished the last estate had?
Is there a backup executor if they can't serve?

options.label: No — I haven't set up my own
options.action: Name your executor and give them the roadmap you didn't have.
steps: Choose an executor (and a backup) and confirm they're willing.
Name them in your will.
Write down where accounts, documents, and passwords live.
Leave the one instruction you most wish the estate you settled had included.

questions._note: DRAFT (2026-07-09): Claude-authored scenario question, matches existing voice/format. Educational situation-surfacing only, not advice. Pending Niki sign-off.
questions.question: After your recent life change, have you re-checked who inherits — beneficiaries, titles, and who's named on your documents?

options.label: Yes — I've updated everything to match
options.action: Review that every document reflects your life as it is now.
checklist: Do your beneficiary designations name the right people today?
Are account and property titles current?
Do your POA and healthcare proxy still name who you'd want?
Does your will reflect the change?

options.label: No — some things may still name the old situation
options.action: Update your beneficiaries and named roles so nothing routes to the wrong person.
steps: List accounts with beneficiary designations (retirement, insurance, bank).
Update each to name the right person after your change.
Re-check property titles and your POA/healthcare proxy.
Update your will if the change affects who inherits.

questions._note: DRAFT (2026-07-09): Claude-authored scenario question, matches existing voice/format. Educational situation-surfacing only, not advice. Pending Niki sign-off.
questions.question: You've hit a milestone age. Do you have the three basics in place — a will, a healthcare proxy, and a financial POA?

options.label: Yes — all three are current
options.action: Review the three basics so they still reflect your wishes.
checklist: Is your will signed and up to date?
Is your healthcare proxy named and on file with your doctor?
Is your financial POA current?
Have any life changes happened since you last updated them?

options.label: I have one or two, not all
options.action: Complete the missing basics so all three are in place.
steps: Note which of will / healthcare proxy / financial POA you have.
Complete the missing one(s) with the right forms for your state.
Store them in your doc box.
Tell your named people where to find them.

options.label: None yet
options.action: Put the three foundational documents in place.
steps: Start with a will (an estate attorney or a reputable service).
Name a healthcare proxy and complete the form for your state.
Set up a financial POA.
Store all three in your doc box and tell your people.

questions._note: DRAFT (2026-07-09): Claude-authored scenario question, matches existing voice/format. Educational situation-surfacing only, not advice. Pending Niki sign-off.
questions.question: Since most of your life is online, is there a single place your trusted person could find every important account and how to reach it?

options.label: Yes — I keep a maintained list / password manager
options.action: Review your digital inventory so it's complete and reachable.
checklist: Does it cover email, banking, photos, social, subscriptions, crypto?
Does your trusted person know it exists and how to unlock it?
Is the recovery/legacy access set up where the platform offers it?
Is it current with accounts you've added recently?

options.label: No — it's scattered across my devices and memory
options.action: Build one digital inventory so nothing important is lost or locked away.
steps: List your key accounts by category (email, money, photos, social, subscriptions).
Put them in one place — a password manager or a single secure document.
Set up legacy/recovery access where the platform offers it.
Tell your trusted person where it lives and how to reach it.

questions._note: DRAFT (2026-07-09): Claude-authored scenario question, matches existing voice/format. Educational situation-surfacing only, not advice. Pending Niki sign-off.
questions.question: A story that shook you usually points at a specific fear. Is the thing you worried could happen to your family actually covered in your plan?

options.label: No — that exact gap is open for me
options.action: Close the specific gap the story exposed, before anything else.
steps: Name the exact thing you feared (guardianship, access, wishes, money).
Check whether your plan currently covers it.
Take the one step that would close it.
Put the result in your doc box.

options.label: I think so, but I want to be sure
options.action: Pressure-test the area the story worried you about.
checklist: What specifically did the story make you afraid of?
Which document or step would prevent that outcome?
Is that document current and findable?
Does your trusted person know about it?

questions._note: DRAFT (2026-07-09): Claude-authored scenario question, matches existing voice/format. Educational situation-surfacing only, not advice. Pending Niki sign-off.
questions.question: You're here because something's been unfinished too long. What's the one thing that's been nagging you?

options.label: A will / who gets what
options.action: Finish the will that's been waiting.
steps: Decide who inherits and who's your executor.
Use an estate attorney or a reputable service to draft it.
Sign it properly for your state.
Store it in your doc box and tell your executor.

options.label: Medical wishes / who decides for me
options.action: Put your healthcare wishes and proxy in place.
steps: Choose who'd make medical decisions if you couldn't.
Complete a healthcare proxy and advance directive for your state.
Talk with your proxy about what matters to you.
Give copies to them, your doctor, and your doc box.

options.label: Access to my accounts / passwords
options.action: Close the access gap that's been open.
steps: Gather your key logins into one secure place.
Set up legacy/recovery access where offered.
Name the person who should have access.
Tell them where it lives.

options.label: Something else
options.action: Name it and take the first concrete step.
steps: Write down the one unfinished thing.
Break it into the very next action.
Do that one action today.
Put whatever results in your doc box.
